medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia for pharyngeal pain to reduce odynophagia and enable small frequent sips of fluid — preferred first-line here because it avoids the renal/GI risk of an NSAID in a patient with reduced fluid intake.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1 g PO every 4-6 hours as needed, maximum 4 g in 24 hours

medications.drug_name: topical throat anaesthetic (benzydamine or lignocaine lozenge/spray)
medications.indication_rationale: A numbing throat spray or lozenge used shortly before drinking reduces swallowing pain enough to allow small frequent sips of electrolyte fluid — the source's specific strategy to protect hydration.
medications.dose_route_frequency: topical to the throat as directed on the product, shortly before attempting to drink

medications.indication_rationale: The source clinician prescribed meloxicam 7.5 mg daily as a short course for anti-inflammatory relief of pharyngeal/lymph-node inflammation. Acceptable as an alternative analgesic ONLY if hydration is maintained and there is no renal contraindication — but paracetamol is preferred here given the dehydration risk.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 7.5 mg PO once daily, short course (as prescribed in the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Reduced oral fluid intake / dehydration risk — NSAIDs increase the risk of pre-renal acute kidney injury when a patient is volume-depleted; avoid or defer until hydration is secure
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: NSAID + reduced fluid intake/dehydration — heightened renal risk in this specific patient; prioritise rehydration and paracetamol first

medications.indication_rationale: Actively NOT indicated — this is a functional/viral post-exertional immune flare with no evidence of bacterial infection (no exudate, no high fever, recurrent identical pattern). Empirical antibiotics are an error of commission; the source note explicitly states no acute antibiotics are indicated.

LDN and supplement strategies are part of longitudinal ME/CFS management to discuss with the treating GP/specialist — they are not an acute-crash intervention and should not be initiated de novo from a telehealth flare consultation.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Some evidence for modest reduction of sore-throat/viral-symptom duration; reasonable if the patient wishes and it does not displace hydration or safety-netting. Not a core requirement.
Listed in the source's targeted strategy; limited evidence in this context. Acceptable as patient preference, must not displace hydration support or safety-netting.
Reasonable to correct a documented deficiency via the GP longitudinally; no acute role in this flare.
A simple comfort measure to soothe the throat; harmless and optional.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take small sips of an oral rehydration/electrolyte solution every 10-15 minutes rather than large drinks; a carer should track roughly how much is taken and when urine was last passed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Frequent small sips are more achievable with painful swallowing and directly counter the main modifiable acute risk (dehydration); urine output is a simple hydration marker for escalation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strict pacing and rest: remain lying flat in a dark, quiet room, avoid all exertion and screens for the next 24-48 hours, and reintroduce activity only very gradually.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Aggressive rest during a post-exertional crash limits further energy depletion and shortens the flare; overexertion worsens and prolongs PEM.

patient_education_points.point: These crashes with a raw sore throat and swollen glands are the body's post-viral flare mimicking the original glandular fever — there is usually no active infection, so antibiotics are not the answer.
Use a numbing throat spray or lozenge just before drinking so you can take small, frequent sips — keeping fluids up is the most important thing tonight.

Because a video call can't look inside the throat, watch closely for danger signs (see the safety-net advice) and act on them straight away — call 000 for any drooling, noisy breathing, inability to swallow saliva, or being unable to wake.
The throat pain and gland swelling usually ease over the next 48-72 hours; getting back to baseline needs strict pacing and rest rather than pushing through.

follow_up_plan.interval: Telehealth GP review within 7 days to monitor the ME/CFS baseline and coordinate longer-term support; sooner (within 24-48 hours) if the throat or hydration is not improving
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Hydration and ability to swallow/drink; trajectory of the post-exertional crash; whether EBV/HIV serology is warranted; consideration of a GP Management Plan (GPMP) for the chronic condition
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If unable to keep any fluids down, reduced urine output or increasing dizziness on standing — same-day in-person review; any airway sign (drooling, noisy breathing, unable to swallow saliva, voice change) or unrousable lethargy — call 000 immediately

must_include_items: Recognise this as a flare of a known recurrent post-viral fatigue condition (recurrent identical post-exertional crash), not a new emergency
Screen airway safety (drooling, ability to swallow saliva, noisy breathing, voice quality, trismus) and hydration status/urine output
Provide analgesia (paracetamol and/or a topical throat anaesthetic) to enable oral fluid intake
Advise small frequent sips of oral rehydration/electrolyte solution and monitoring of urine output
Give explicit, time-bound safety-netting: 000 for any airway sign or unrousable lethargy; same-day/ED if unable to keep fluids down or reduced urine output
Arrange prompt GP (telehealth) follow-up for the chronic condition rather than emergency escalation as the baseline
acceptable_alternatives: An NSAID (e.g. meloxicam) as anti-inflammatory analgesia IS acceptable instead of/alongside paracetamol IF hydration is maintained and there is no renal contraindication — but paracetamol is preferred given the dehydration risk
Arranging a same-day in-person review (T3) is an acceptable more-cautious disposition given the inability to examine the throat remotely — it should not be scored as an error
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net the airway (drooling/stridor/inability to swallow saliva/voice change) with a 000 instruction
Failing to address hydration / oral rehydration and urine-output monitoring
Failing to recognise the recurrent chronic flare pattern and anchoring on the acute throat as a new emergency
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antibiotics for a functional/viral immune flare with no bacterial evidence
Over-triaging the baseline to emergency ambulance (000) or ED when the airway is currently safe and the acute red-flag screen is negative
Recommending an NSAID without accounting for the dehydration/renal risk
Dismissing the throat entirely and missing the need to safety-net the unexcludable quinsy/epiglottitis
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) NOT over-triaging the baseline to ED/000 while (b) delivering explicit airway AND hydration safety-netting with time bounds, plus analgesia + hydration support and prompt GP follow-up. Blanket emergency escalation of a currently-safe airway, or empirical antibiotics, is the scored defect; equally, dismissing the throat with no airway/hydration safety-net is an unsafe under-triage.
